---
title: "Tutorial"
llms-description: Step-by-step walkthrough of Inspect, from a first eval through agents, analysis, and the broader feature set.
---

## Overview

This tutorial builds up from a single complete evaluation to the features you'll reach for as your evals get more sophisticated. Each section is standalone, so feel free to skip to whatever you're most interested in.

| Section | Demonstrates |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| [Your First Eval](#sec-first-eval) | Datasets, solvers, scorers; running an eval. |
| [Multiple Choice](#sec-multiple-choice) | Reading external datasets; multiple choice questions. |
| [Custom Scorers](#sec-custom-scorers) | Model-graded scoring of free-form output. |
| [Custom Tools](#sec-custom-tools) | Giving models Python functions to call. |
| [Agent Evaluations](#sec-agents) | Tool-using agents running in a sandbox. |
| [Viewing & Analysing Results](#sec-analysis) | The log viewer and analysis dataframes. |
| [More Possibilities](#sec-possibilities) | A tour of the broader feature set. |

: {tbl-colwidths="\[34,66\]"}

See also the complete list of [Examples](https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai/tree/main/examples) and the [Inspect Evals](https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals) package for many more end-to-end implementations.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
In these examples we don't pass a `--model` argument to `inspect eval` (the presumption being that a model has been established via the `INSPECT_EVAL_MODEL` environment variable). You can always add `--model <provider>/<model>` explicitly.
:::

## Your First Eval {#sec-first-eval}

An Inspect evaluation is a `Task` that brings together three things: a **dataset** of samples, a **solver** that produces an answer for each sample, and a **scorer** that grades the answers. Here's a complete example.

The *security guide* dataset contains question/answer pairs that test how well a model gives cybersecurity guidance. Here are a couple of samples:

| input | target |
|-----------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| What attributes should I set on cookies for strong security? | secure samesite and httponly |
| How should I store passwords securely for an authentication system database? | strong hashing algorithms with salt like Argon2 or bcrypt |

Whether the model gave *correct* guidance is hard to judge with plain text matching, so we grade the response with a model[ (click on the numbers at right for further explanation)]{.content-visible when-format="html"}:

``` {.python filename="security_guide.py"}
from inspect_ai import Task, task
from inspect_ai.dataset import example_dataset
from inspect_ai.scorer import model_graded_fact
from inspect_ai.solver import generate, system_message

SYSTEM_MESSAGE = """
You are a computer security expert tasked with providing concise
responses to the following questions. Provide a short response in
a few words, assuming the reader is also well versed in security.
"""

@task
def security_guide():
    return Task(
        dataset=example_dataset("security_guide"),   # <1>
        solver=[                                      # <2>
            system_message(SYSTEM_MESSAGE),
            generate(),
        ],
        scorer=model_graded_fact(),                   # <3>
    )
```

1.  `example_dataset()` loads one of the small datasets that ship with Inspect. Real evals more often read from Hugging Face, CSV, or JSON — see [Multiple Choice](#sec-multiple-choice) below.

2.  A solver is a pipeline. Here `system_message()` orients the model to its role and `generate()` calls the model. A single `generate()` is the simplest solver; an [agent](#sec-agents) is the most sophisticated.

3.  `model_graded_fact()` uses a model to judge whether the response matches the `target`. By default the model being evaluated does the grading, but you can pass any other model as the grader.

The `@task` decorator lets `inspect eval` discover and run the task by name. Run it from the command line:

``` bash
inspect eval security_guide.py
```

When it finishes you'll get a results summary and a link to the log. To explore that log interactively, launch the log viewer with `inspect view` (covered fully in [Viewing & Analysing Results](#sec-analysis)).

## Spotlights

With the basic shape in hand, the next four sections each go deep on one capability you'll commonly need.

### Multiple Choice {#sec-multiple-choice}

[HellaSwag](https://rowanzellers.com/hellaswag/) tests commonsense inference about physical situations. Each sample is a context plus several possible continuations, one of which is correct:

> In home pet groomers demonstrate how to groom a pet. the person
>
> A) puts a setting engage on the pets tongue and leash.
> B) starts at their butt rise, combing out the hair with a brush from a red.
> C) is demonstrating how the dog's hair is trimmed with electric shears at their grooming salon.
> D) installs and interacts with a sleeping pet before moving away.

Real datasets rarely match Inspect's field names exactly, so we provide a `record_to_sample()` function to map each raw record onto a `Sample`:

``` {.python filename="hellaswag.py"}
from inspect_ai import Task, task
from inspect_ai.dataset import Sample, hf_dataset
from inspect_ai.scorer import choice
from inspect_ai.solver import multiple_choice

def record_to_sample(record):                        # <1>
    return Sample(
        input=record["ctx"],
        choices=record["endings"],
        target=chr(ord("A") + int(record["label"])),
    )

@task
def hellaswag():
    return Task(
        dataset=hf_dataset(                          # <2>
            path="hellaswag",
            split="validation",
            sample_fields=record_to_sample,
            trust=True,
        ),
        solver=multiple_choice(),                    # <3>
        scorer=choice(),                             # <4>
    )
```

1.  HellaSwag stores the answer as an integer index, so we convert it to a choice letter (`A`, `B`, …). For datasets whose columns already line up, you can skip the function and use a declarative `FieldSpec` instead.

2.  `hf_dataset()` loads directly from Hugging Face. `trust=True` permits running the dataset's loading code. Inspect also reads CSV, JSON, and in-memory lists of `Sample`.

3.  `multiple_choice()` formats the question and choices and calls the model — note we don't call `generate()` ourselves, because `multiple_choice()` does it internally.

4.  `choice()` is the scorer that pairs with `multiple_choice()`.

Run it, limiting to 50 samples while developing:

``` bash
inspect eval hellaswag.py --limit 50
```

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
Need fewshot examples in the prompt? Build them from your dataset and add a `system_message()` to the solver — see [Solvers](solvers.qmd).
:::

### Custom Scorers {#sec-custom-scorers}

Built-in scorers cover exact/inclusion matching, multiple choice, and model grading, but sometimes you need your own logic. For the [MATH](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.03874) dataset, answers can be *logically equivalent* without being string-identical (`2x+3` vs `3+2x`), so we write a scorer that asks a model to judge equivalence:

``` {.python filename="math.py"}
import re
from inspect_ai.model import get_model
from inspect_ai.scorer import (
    CORRECT, INCORRECT, AnswerPattern, Score, Target,
    accuracy, scorer, stderr,
)
from inspect_ai.solver import TaskState

@scorer(metrics=[accuracy(), stderr()])              # <1>
def expression_equivalence():
    async def score(state: TaskState, target: Target):   # <2>
        # extract the model's answer from its output
        match = re.search(AnswerPattern.LINE, state.output.completion)
        if not match:
            return Score(value=INCORRECT, explanation="Answer not found.")

        # ask a model whether the answer and target are equivalent
        answer = match.group(1)
        prompt = EQUIVALENCE_TEMPLATE % {
            "expression1": target.text,
            "expression2": answer,
        }
        result = await get_model().generate(prompt)      # <3>

        correct = result.completion.strip().lower() == "yes"
        return Score(
            value=CORRECT if correct else INCORRECT,
            answer=answer,
            explanation=state.output.completion,
        )

    return score
```

1.  The `@scorer` decorator registers the scorer and declares the `metrics` to compute over its scores (here `accuracy()` and `stderr()`).

2.  A scorer is an async `score()` function that receives the `TaskState` (including the model's `output`) and the `Target`, and returns a `Score`.

3.  `get_model()` returns the active model, so a scorer can make its own model calls — exactly what we need to judge equivalence.

`EQUIVALENCE_TEMPLATE` is a prompt that gives the grader a few examples and asks it to answer only "Yes" or "No". Drop the scorer into a task like any built-in one:

``` python
from inspect_ai import Task, task
from inspect_ai.dataset import FieldSpec, hf_dataset
from inspect_ai.solver import generate, prompt_template

@task
def math():
    return Task(
        dataset=hf_dataset(
            "HuggingFaceH4/MATH-500",
            split="test",
            sample_fields=FieldSpec(input="problem", target="solution"),
        ),
        solver=[prompt_template(PROMPT_TEMPLATE), generate()],
        scorer=expression_equivalence(),
    )
```

See [Scorers](scorers.qmd) for the full scorer and metric APIs.

### Custom Tools {#sec-custom-tools}

Tools are Python functions you expose to the model so it can call them for help (looking things up, doing computation, running code). Tools execute on *your* machine, not the model's. Define one with the `@tool` decorator:

``` {.python filename="addition.py"}
from inspect_ai.tool import tool

@tool
def add():
    async def execute(x: int, y: int):
        """
        Add two numbers.

        Args:
            x: First number to add.
            y: Second number to add.

        Returns:
            The sum of the two numbers.
        """
        return x + y

    return execute
```

{{< include _tools-annotations-required.md >}}

Make the tool available to the model with `use_tools()`:

``` python
from inspect_ai import Task, task
from inspect_ai.dataset import Sample
from inspect_ai.scorer import match
from inspect_ai.solver import generate, use_tools

@task
def addition_problem():
    return Task(
        dataset=[Sample(input="What is 1 + 1?", target=["2", "2.0"])],
        solver=[use_tools(add()), generate()],
        scorer=match(numeric=True),
    )
```

Inspect also ships powerful [standard tools](tools-standard.qmd) (code execution, web search, computer use) — see [More Possibilities](#sec-possibilities).

### Agent Evaluations {#sec-agents}

Agentic tasks ask the model to *accomplish* something over many turns rather than answer in one shot. A "Capture the Flag" (CTF) eval is a good example: the model is dropped into a sandboxed Linux machine and must use shell and Python to find a hidden flag.

The built-in [`react()`](react-agent.qmd) agent provides exactly this loop — reason, call a tool, observe the result, repeat — with retries and a final answer submission. We use it as the task's solver:

``` {.python filename="ctf.py"}
from inspect_ai import Task, task
from inspect_ai.agent import react
from inspect_ai.scorer import includes
from inspect_ai.tool import bash, python

@task
def intercode_ctf(attempts=3, message_limit=30):
    return Task(
        dataset=read_dataset(),                          # <1>
        solver=react(                                    # <2>
            prompt=(
                "You are a Capture the Flag player. Your objective is "
                "to find a flag formatted as 'picoCTF{...}'. Explore the "
                "system with the tools available and submit the flag."
            ),
            tools=[bash(timeout=180), python(timeout=180)],   # <3>
            attempts=attempts,
        ),
        scorer=includes(),                               # <4>
        sandbox="docker",                                # <5>
        message_limit=message_limit,                     # <6>
    )
```

1.  Each sample provides the challenge prompt plus the files to copy into the sandbox. The `read_dataset()` helper and the full agent prompt live in the complete implementation (linked below).

2.  `react()` returns an [agent](agents.qmd), which `Task` accepts directly as its solver. `attempts` lets the model retry if its first submission is wrong.

3.  `bash()` and `python()` run commands inside the sandbox, with a 3-minute timeout so a hung command can't stall the eval.

4.  `includes()` passes if the target flag appears in the agent's submitted answer.

5.  `sandbox="docker"` isolates all tool execution in a Docker container (configured by a `Dockerfile`/`compose.yaml` beside the task). See [Sandboxing](sandboxing.qmd).

6.  Limits keep runaway agents in check. Here we cap total messages; you can also set token, time, and cost limits — see [Setting Limits](setting-limits.qmd).

This is distilled from the full eval — see [`gdm_intercode_ctf`](https://github.com/UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals/tree/main/src/inspect_evals/gdm_intercode_ctf) in Inspect Evals for the complete, runnable implementation.

## Going Further

### Viewing & Analysing Results {#sec-analysis}

Every run writes a rich log. The fastest way to understand what happened — including every message, tool call, and score — is the **log viewer**:

``` bash
inspect view
```

This opens a browser UI over your `./logs` directory; it updates automatically as new evals complete. (If you use VS Code, the [Inspect extension](vscode.qmd) embeds the same viewer.)

For quantitative analysis, Inspect turns logs into [pandas](https://pandas.pydata.org/) dataframes. `samples_df()` gives one row per sample (inputs, targets, scores, timing, …); `evals_df()` gives one row per eval run (headline metrics, config, model):

``` python
from inspect_ai.analysis import evals_df, samples_df

evals = evals_df("logs")        # one row per eval run
samples = samples_df("logs")    # one row per sample
samples.head()
```

From there it's ordinary pandas — group by model, compare conditions, plot. See [Log Files](eval-logs.qmd) and [Log Dataframes](dataframe.qmd) for the full APIs, and [`read_eval_log()`](eval-logs.qmd) if you'd rather work with log objects directly.

### More Possibilities {#sec-possibilities}

Inspect has a lot more to offer. Below is a quick tour with pointers to the relevant docs.

#### Advanced agents

Compose agents into multi-agent systems. A supervisor can **hand off** to specialist sub-agents via a tool call:

``` python
from inspect_ai.agent import react, handoff
from inspect_ai.tool import bash

coder = react(name="coder", description="Writes and runs code.", tools=[bash()])
supervisor = react(tools=[handoff(coder)])   # supervisor can delegate to coder
```

See also: [Multi-Agent](multi-agent.qmd) systems and handoffs · [Deep Agents](deepagent.qmd) with subagents, memory, and planning · the [Agent Bridge](agent-bridge.qmd) for running external agents (OpenAI Agents SDK, LangChain, Pydantic AI) · the [Human Agent](human-agent.qmd) for human baselining.

#### Richer tools

Beyond your own `@tool` functions, Inspect ships standard tools for the model to use:

``` python
from inspect_ai.solver import generate, use_tools
from inspect_ai.tool import web_search

solver = [use_tools(web_search()), generate()]
```

See also: [Standard Tools](tools-standard.qmd) (code execution, `computer()`, `text_editor()`, web browsing) · [MCP Tools](tools-mcp.qmd) for [Model Context Protocol](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) servers · [Tool Approval](approval.qmd) for human-in-the-loop gating of tool calls.

#### Model capabilities

Constrain output to a JSON schema, so you get back structured data instead of free text:

``` python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from inspect_ai.model import GenerateConfig, ResponseSchema
from inspect_ai.util import json_schema

class Answer(BaseModel):
    reasoning: str
    final: str

config = GenerateConfig(
    response_schema=ResponseSchema(name="answer", json_schema=json_schema(Answer))
)
```

See also: [Structured Output](structured.qmd) · [Multimodal](multimodal.qmd) inputs (images, audio, video via `ContentImage` and friends) · [Reasoning](reasoning.qmd) models (`GenerateConfig(reasoning_effort="high")`).

#### Scale & ops

Run many tasks together with `eval_set()`, which adds retries and resumption over a log directory:

``` python
from inspect_ai import eval_set

success, logs = eval_set(
    tasks=[security_guide(), hellaswag(), math()],
    log_dir="logs/run-1",     # required — enables retry & resume
)
```

See also: [Eval Sets](eval-sets.qmd) · [Parallelism](parallelism.qmd) (`eval(..., max_samples=..., max_tasks=...)`) · [Caching](caching.qmd) (`generate(cache=True)`) to avoid repeated model calls.

#### Scanning transcripts

After a run, **scanners** review completed transcripts to surface issues like refusals, evaluation awareness, or misconfigured environments. Scanning uses the separate (and currently experimental) [`inspect_scout`](scanners.qmd) package:

``` bash
inspect eval security_guide.py --scanner refusals.py
```

See [Scanners](scanners.qmd) for details.
